unit.name: Greedy and beam decoding under repetition pressure
unit.description: Explains why maximizing model probability works for constrained tasks but degenerates for open-ended generation.
unit.summary: The segment defines decoding as the function that chooses a token from the model's next-token distribution [ts=915s]. It reviews greedy decoding and beam search as maximum-probability methods aimed at finding the most likely string, which is sensible for low-entropy tasks such as translation and summarization [ts=934s] [ts=968s]. The lecture then shows why this objective fails for open-ended generation: the most likely continuations become repetitive, and plots reveal a self-amplification effect in which repeated phrases receive increasingly favorable probability or lower negative log-likelihood as repetition continues [ts=1008s] [ts=1039s]. This degeneration appears in both LSTMs and transformers and does not disappear just by scaling to larger models [ts=1070s]. N-gram blocking is introduced as a simple heuristic, but it can wrongly ban legitimate reuse such as names appearing multiple times [ts=1098s]. The broader takeaway is that maximum-probability decoding does not match human uncertainty patterns, since human text shows fluctuating confidence while beam-decoded text is unrealistically overconfident [ts=1232s].

key_points.text: Greedy decoding chooses the highest-probability token at each time step, while beam search keeps multiple candidate continuations.
Maximum-probability decoding is suitable for low-entropy tasks but encounters problems on open-ended generation.
Repeated phrases can become more probable as they repeat, revealing a self-amplification repetition effect.
N-gram blocking prevents exact repeated n-grams but can incorrectly ban valid reuse such as repeated names.
Human token probabilities fluctuate more than beam-decoded text, suggesting argmax-style decoding mismatches human generation patterns.

unit_summary_for_generation: The segment defines decoding as the function that chooses a token from the model's next-token distribution [ts=915s]. It reviews greedy decoding and beam search as maximum-probability methods aimed at finding the most likely string, which is sensible for low-entropy tasks such as translation and summarization [ts=934s] [ts=968s]. The lecture then shows why this objective fails for open-ended generation: the most likely continuations become repetitive, and plots reveal a self-amplification effect in which repeated phrases receive increasingly favorable probability or lower negative log-likelihood as repetition continues [ts=1008s] [ts=1039s]. This degeneration appears in both LSTMs and transformers and does not disappear just by scaling to larger models [ts=1070s]. N-gram blocking is introduced as a simple heuristic, but it can wrongly ban legitimate reuse such as names appearing multiple times [ts=1098s]. The broader takeaway is that maximum-probability decoding does not match human uncertainty patterns, since human text shows fluctuating confidence while beam-decoded text is unrealistically overconfident [ts=1232s].

kp_catalog.name: Degeneration under maximum-probability decoding
kp_catalog.description: The failure mode where greedy or beam-style search produces repetitive, overconfident text in open-ended generation.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because degeneration under maximum-probability decoding clarifies the failure mode where greedy or beam-style search produces repetitive, overconfident text in open-ended generation, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Autoregressive language modeling
kp_catalog.description: Training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly. Autoregressive models make sequence modeling tractable by factoring p(x) into conditional next-part distributions over an ordered decomposition of x.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because autoregressive language modeling formalizes how training a model to predict the next token from its prefix, used directly for decoder pretraining and as a baseline pretraining objective more broadly, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context while roles split between gateway and supporting and importance labels split between critical and high.
